meta.title: Why & How We Prototype
meta.subtitle: Prototyping in digital service design serves many purposes — from exploring what a digital material can do, to testing whether an approach is viable, feasible, and desirable. Each prototype carries a different mix of these intentions.
meta.note: Click or hover on nodes to explore connections. Drag nodes to reposition. Press D to dump positions to console, S to export SVG, P to export PNG.

Buxton, 2007.title: Sketching User Experiences: Getting the Design Right and the Right Design

Zimmerman et al., 2007.title: Research Through Design as a Method for Interaction Design Research in HCI

Wiberg, 2014.title: Interaction Design and the Concept of 'Material'

Knapp et al., 2016.title: Sprint: How to Solve Big Problems and Test New Ideas in Just Five Days

Sterling, 2009.title: Design Fiction: A Short Essay on Design, Science, Fact and Fiction

nodes.label:
Why & How
We Prototype
nodes.description: The central question. Every prototype is made for a reason — to explore, to evaluate, to communicate, to provoke. The mix of purposes shapes what you build and how you build it. A prototype is not a miniature product; it is an instrument of inquiry (Lim et al., 2008).

nodes.label: Desirability
nodes.description: Does anyone want this? Prototyping for desirability means putting something in front of people to see whether it resonates — whether it solves a real problem, fits into their lives, or sparks genuine interest (Brown, 2009).

nodes.label: Feasibility
nodes.description: Can we build this? Feasibility prototypes test whether an approach is technically achievable — whether the technology works, the data is available, and the system can perform as imagined (Houde & Hill, 1997).

nodes.label: Viability
nodes.description: Should we build this? Viability prototypes test whether an idea makes sense as a business or organizational proposition — whether it can be sustained, funded, and delivered at scale (Ries, 2011; Brown, 2009).

nodes.label: Exploration
nodes.description: Explorative prototyping opens up the design space. You build to discover what questions to ask, not to answer ones you already have. Sketches are the quintessential explorative tool — they suggest and provoke rather than describe and specify (Buxton, 2007).

nodes.label: Evaluation
nodes.description: Evaluative prototyping narrows the design space. You build to test a specific hypothesis or compare alternatives. The prototype becomes an instrument for gathering evidence — from usability tests to A/B experiments (Knapp et al., 2016).

nodes.label:
Research
Through Design
nodes.description: Prototyping as a way of producing knowledge, not just products. Making things is itself a research method — the artifact embodies knowledge that cannot be expressed in words alone (Zimmerman et al., 2007; Koskinen et al., 2011).

nodes.label:
Speculative
Prototyping
nodes.description: Prototypes that provoke rather than solve. Speculative and critical design uses artifacts to ask 'what if?' — imagining alternative futures and surfacing hidden assumptions about technology and society (Dunne & Raby, 2013; Sterling, 2009).

nodes.label:
Filtering
Dimensions
nodes.description: Every prototype selectively manifests some qualities while leaving others unresolved. Choosing what to include and exclude is a design decision — the 'anatomy' of a prototype determines what you can learn from it (Lim et al., 2008).

nodes.label:
Digital
Material
nodes.description: Code, data, APIs, and connectivity are design materials with their own properties and affordances. Prototyping with digital materials means exploring what computation can do — not just implementing a predetermined idea (Vallgårda & Redström, 2007; Wiberg, 2014).

nodes.label:
Code as
Design Practice
nodes.description: When designers write code, they are not merely implementing — they are designing. Programming becomes a way of thinking through material possibilities, discovering what the technology affords (Löwgren, 2016).

nodes.label:
Computational
Composites
nodes.description: Digital artifacts are composites of physical form and computational behavior. Prototyping these composites requires working across hardware, software, and interaction — giving form to things that change over time (Vallgårda & Redström, 2007).

nodes.label:
Paper
Prototype
nodes.description: The simplest and fastest way to test an interaction concept. Paper prototypes are disposable, inviting, and accessible to non-designers. Their low fidelity is a feature: it keeps attention on structure and flow rather than visual polish (Snyder, 2003; Rettig, 1994).

nodes.label:
Wizard
of Oz
nodes.description: A human secretly performs the system's role. Wizard of Oz prototypes let you test complex behavior — AI responses, voice interfaces, adaptive systems — before building any of it. They decouple desirability testing from technical implementation.

nodes.label:
Experience
Prototype
nodes.description: Prototyping the subjective experience of using a service or product, not just the interface. Uses bodystorming, role-play, and staged scenarios to make the future experience tangible and testable (Buchenau & Suri, 2000).

nodes.label:
Service
Prototype
nodes.description: Prototyping a service means staging the whole journey — touchpoints, backstage processes, human interactions over time. From desktop walkthroughs to live pilots, service prototypes capture temporal and relational qualities that product prototypes miss (Blomkvist, 2014; Stickdorn et al., 2018).

nodes.label:
Minimum
Viable Product
nodes.description: The smallest thing you can release to learn whether the market wants it. MVPs test viability through real-world exposure, but risk conflating a prototype (a learning instrument) with a product (a value proposition) (Ries, 2011; Savoia, 2011).

nodes.label:
Design
Sprint
nodes.description: A structured five-day process: understand, sketch, decide, prototype, test. The Sprint method compresses the exploration-to-evaluation cycle into one week, forcing 'just enough' prototype fidelity to learn from users (Knapp et al., 2016).

nodes.label:
Low
Fidelity
nodes.description: Quick, rough, disposable. Low-fidelity prototypes invite critique and iteration. Their incompleteness is deliberate — it signals that everything is open to change and encourages participants to focus on concept rather than detail (Buxton, 2007).

nodes.label:
High
Fidelity
nodes.description: Detailed, polished, close to final. High-fidelity prototypes answer questions about specific interactions, visual design, and technical behavior. But they risk anchoring the team on a particular solution too early (Houde & Hill, 1997).

nodes.label:
Role / Look
& Feel / Impl.
nodes.description: Houde and Hill's framework: prototypes explore 'role' (what it does for the user), 'look and feel' (sensory experience), or 'implementation' (how it works technically). No single prototype covers all three — choosing which to foreground is a strategic decision (Houde & Hill, 1997).
